does: This paper addresses reward hacking in text-to-image diffusion models during inference-time alignment. The authors identify that existing noise-space regularization methods fail to prevent the model from generating images that maximize scalar rewards like aesthetic scores while deviating from the original text prompt. They propose MIRA, a training-free method that introduces an image-space, score-based KL surrogate to regularize the diffusion sampling trajectory. This constraint keeps the output distribution close to the original model's distribution while allowing reward improvement, preventing off-distribution drift. The method operates entirely at inference time without requiring model fine-tuning, making it computationally efficient while maintaining fidelity to user prompts.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in inference-time alignment for diffusion models, extending methods that optimize initial noise to steer the denoising process toward higher rewards. It directly responds to limitations in prior noise-space regularization approaches that fail to prevent reward hacking. The paper connects to broader research on aligning generative models with human preferences and scalar reward functions, an increasingly important problem as diffusion models become deployed for user-facing applications. It also draws on score-based diffusion theory and KL regularization techniques from the literature, adapting them to the image space rather than noise space. The work relates to general challenges in reinforcement learning and reward optimization where maximizing a proxy objective can lead to unintended behavior.
enables: MIRA opens pathways for more robust inference-time alignment of generative models without computational overhead of fine-tuning. It enables practitioners to align diffusion models with diverse reward signals—aesthetic quality, object detection, style matching—while preserving prompt adherence, broadening practical applications. The image-space regularization framework could extend to other conditional generation tasks and reward criteria beyond T2I models. The method suggests future research into better characterizing and preventing distribution shift in reward-guided generation, potentially informing alignment techniques for other generative models. It also points toward developing more sophisticated reward functions that better capture user intent, since purely scalar rewards remain vulnerable to hacking. The training-free nature makes this approach accessible for practitioners and could facilitate safer deployment of powerful generative models in real-world settings.
